summary: Reporter finds the closing </svg> tag missing when using SKSvgCanvas with SKManagedWStream, because managedStream.ToArray() is called before the SKCanvas is disposed — the SVG document is finalized (closing tag written) on canvas disposal, so reading the stream before disposal yields incomplete output.

stepsToReproduce: Create a MemoryStream, wrap it in SKManagedWStream, create SKSvgCanvas with the stream
Call managedStream.ToArray() to read the SVG data (while canvas is still alive)
Observe the closing </svg> tag is missing
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 3.119.0 on Blazor + iOS via Rider, also reproduces on all browsers in release builds

screenshots.description: SVG renders correctly after manually adding closing tag

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: This is not a version regression — the SVG canvas has always written the closing tag on disposal. The reporter's code reads the stream before the canvas is disposed, which would produce the same incomplete output in any version. The reporter likely had different disposal ordering in their 2.88.x code.

regression.reason: The Skia SVG canvas has always written the closing tag on canvas destruction. The C API sk_svgcanvas_create_with_stream returns an SkSVGCanvas::Make unique_ptr that finalizes the document on destruction. The reporter's 2.88.x code likely had correct disposal ordering (scoped using blocks) and the 3.x refactor changed to using-var declarations without preserving the ordering.

analysis.summary: The SVG canvas writes the closing </svg> tag when the canvas is disposed (via SkCanvas destructor in Skia). The reporter's code uses 'using var' declarations, which defer disposal to the end of the enclosing scope. They call managedStream.ToArray() before the canvas disposes, so the closing tag hasn't been written yet. The existing test SvgCanvasSavesFile demonstrates the correct pattern: use a scoped using block for the canvas, then read the stream after the block ends.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter genuinely encountered broken behavior, but the root cause is incorrect disposal ordering in the user's code, not a SkiaSharp defect. The SVG canvas finalizing on disposal is by-design Skia behavior. Suggesting close-as-by-design with a workaround showing the correct pattern, matching the existing test code.

keySignals.text: using var stream = new SKManagedWStream(managedStream); using var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, stream); ... result.Data = managedStream.ToArray();
keySignals.interpretation: managedStream.ToArray() is called while the canvas is still alive — the SVG document hasn't been finalized yet.

keySignals.text: it might be SKManagedWStream specific. SKFileWStream seems to output a correct SVG with close tags.
keySignals.interpretation: With SKFileWStream the reporter likely reads the file after all using blocks complete, so the canvas is disposed first and the closing tag is present.

keySignals.text: This seems to be a 'recent' issue as it certainly used to work
keySignals.interpretation: Not a code regression — likely a change in disposal ordering when migrating to using-var declarations in the reporter's code.

codeInvestigation.finding: The Stream overload creates an internal SKManagedWStream and uses SKObject.Owned to tie its lifetime to the canvas. The SKWStream overload uses SKObject.Referenced. In both cases, the SVG document is finalized when the canvas (SkSVGCanvas in C++) is destroyed.

sk_svgcanvas_create_with_stream calls SkSVGCanvas::Make().release(). The unique_ptr destructor writes the closing </svg> tag. This means the closing tag is only written when sk_canvas_destroy is called (canvas disposal).
DisposeNative calls sk_canvas_destroy, which triggers the Skia SVG canvas destructor that writes the closing </svg> tag to the stream.

The SvgCanvasSavesFile test demonstrates the correct pattern: uses a scoped 'using' block for the canvas, then reads the MemoryStream AFTER the block ends. This ensures the closing tag is written before reading.

workarounds: Dispose the SVG canvas before reading the stream — use a scoped using block instead of using-var declaration
Use the Stream overload of SKSvgCanvas.Create (pass MemoryStream directly) to simplify the code

resolution.hypothesis: The reporter's code reads the MemoryStream before the SVG canvas is disposed. The SVG closing tag is written by Skia's SkSVGCanvas destructor, which runs on canvas disposal. This is by-design behavior, not a SkiaSharp bug.

proposals.title: Use scoped using block for canvas
proposals.description: Restructure the code to use a scoped using block for the SKSvgCanvas so the canvas is disposed (and the SVG document finalized) before reading the stream data.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Use scoped using block for canvas
resolution.recommendedReason: Trivial code change that exactly matches the pattern used in SkiaSharp's own test suite (SvgCanvasSavesFile). Also removes the unnecessary explicit SKManagedWStream since SKSvgCanvas.Create has a Stream overload.

actionability.reason: The SVG canvas writes the closing tag on disposal by design. The reporter's code reads the stream before disposal. The correct pattern is demonstrated in the project's own test suite.

actions.description: Apply bug, SkiaSharp, and SVG backend labels
Explain the disposal ordering issue and provide the correct pattern
Close as by-design — disposal ordering is correct Skia behavior
